law-the-divorce-act-1869.title: Understanding The Divorce Act, 1869
law-the-divorce-act-1869.desc: A structured course on The Divorce Act, 1869 (1869). Study key provisions, rights, obligations, and enforcement mechanisms section by section. Ideal for law students, legal professionals, and informed citizens seeking to understand Family Law in Pakistan.
law-the-divorce-act-1869.overview: The Divorce Act, 1869 (1869) is a key piece of legislation under Family Law in Pakistan. This course offers a comprehensive, section-by-section study of the law, covering provisions on short title, commencement of act, extent of act, interpretation-clause. The law is binding across Pakistan and plays a critical role in shaping legal rights, obligations, and procedures in this domain. Upon completing this course, learners will be able to understand and apply the statutory framework, identify rights and duties, and navigate legal situations arising under this legislation with confidence.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
1. 
Short title, Commencement of Act

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Divorce Act, 1869. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. lessons.title: Interpretation-clause
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Interpretation-clause' under The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 addresses 'Interpretation-clause'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
In this Act, unless there is something repugnant in the subject or context,- "Church of England" and "Anglican" mean and apply to the Church of England as by law established; "Church of Scotland" means the Church of Scotland as by law established; "Church of Rome" and "Roman Catholic" mean and apply to the Church which regards the Pope of Rome as its spiritual head; "Church" includes any chapel or other building generally used for public Christian worship; "minor" means a person who has not completed the age of twenty-one years and who is not a widower or a widow; the expression "Christians" means persons professing the Christian religion; and the expression "Native Christians" includes the Christian descendants of Natives of Indo-Pakistan subcontinent converted to Christianity, as well as such converts; "Registrar General of Births, Deaths and Marriages" means a Registrar General of Births, Deaths and Marriages appointed under the Births, Deaths and Marriages Registration Act, 1886 (VI of 1886).

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a couple in Lahore planning their wedding ceremony. In this real-world scenario, 'Interpretation-clause' of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

"Church of Scotland" means the Church of Scotland as by law established
"Church of Rome" and "Roman Catholic" mean and apply to the Church which regards the Pope of Rome as its spiritual head
"Church" includes any chapel or other building generally used for public Christian worship

lessons.title: Marriages to be solemnized according to Act
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Marriages to be solemnized according to Act' under The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 addresses 'Marriages to be solemnized according to Act'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Every marriage between persons, one or both of whom is or are a Christian or Christians, shall be solemnized in accordance with the provisions of the next following section; and any such marriage solemnized otherwise than in accordance with such provisions shall be void.

lessons.title: Persons by whom marriages may be solemnized
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Persons by whom marriages may be solemnized' under The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 addresses 'Persons by whom marriages may be solemnized'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Marriages may be solemnized in Pakistan. (1) by any person who has received episcopal ordination, provided that the marriage be solemnized according to the rules, rites, ceremonies and customs of the Church of which he is a Minister; (2) by any Clergyman of the Church of the Scotland, provided that such marriage be solemnized according to the rules, rites, ceremonies and customs of the Church of Scotland (3) by any Minister of Religion licensed under this Act to solemnize marriages; (4) by; or in the presence of, a Marriage Registrar appointed under this Act; (5) by any person licensed under this Act to grant certificates of marriage between Native Christians.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a couple in Lahore planning their wedding ceremony. In this real-world scenario, 'Persons by whom marriages may be solemnized' of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

This section of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 addresses 'Grant and revocation of licenses to solemnize marriage'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

The Provincial Government may appoint one or more Christians, either by name or as holding any office for the time being, to be the Marriage Registrar or Marriage Registrars for any district subject to its administration. Senior Marriage Registrar. Where there are more Marriage Registrars than one in any district, the provincial Government shall appoint one of them to be the Senior Marriage Registrar. Magistrate when to be Marriage Registrar. When there is only one Marriage Registrar in a district, and such Registrar is absent from such district, or ill, or when his office is temporarily vacant, the Magistrate of the district shall act as, and be, Marriage Registrar thereof during such absence, illness or temporary vacancy.

lessons.title: Time for solemnizing marriage
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Time for solemnizing marriage' under The Christian Marriage Act, 1872. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Christian Marriage Act, 1872 addresses 'Time for solemnizing marriage'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Every marriage under this Act shall be solemnized between the hours of six in the morning and seven in the evening: Exceptions. Provided that nothing in this section shall apply to (1) a Clergyman of the Church of England solemnizing a marriage under a special license permitting him to do so at any hour other than between six in the morning and seven in the evening, under the hand and seal of the Anglican Bishop of the Diocese or his Commissary, or (2) a Clergyman of the Church of Rome solemnizing a marriage between the hours of seven in the evening and six in the morning, when he has received a general or special license in that behalf from the Roman Catholic Bishop of the Diocese or Vicariate in which such marriage is so solemnized, or from such person as the same Bishop has authorized to grant such license, or a Clergyman of the Church of Scotland solemnizing a marriage according to the rules, rites, ceremonies and customs of the Church of Scotland.

INTRODUCTION

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
In this Act, unless there is something repugnant in the subject or context,-
(1) "minor" means a person who, under the provisions of the Majority Act, 1875 (IX of 1875), is to be deemed not to have attained his majority:
(2) "guardian" means a person having the care of the person of a minor or of his property, or of both his person and property:
(3) "ward" means a minor for whose person or property, or both, there is a guardian:
(4) "District Court" has the meaning assigned to that expression in the 1[Code of Civil Procedure, 1908] (Act V of 1908) and includes a High Court in the exercise of its ordinary original civil jurisdiction:
2[(5) "the Court" means:-
(a) the District Court having jurisdiction to entertain an application under this Act for an order appointing or declaring a person to be a guardian; or
(b) where a guardian has been appointed or declared in pursuance of any such application-
(i) the Court which, or the Court of the Officer who, appointed or declared the guardian or is under this Act deemed to have appointed or declared the guardian; or
(ii) in any matter relating to the person of the ward the District Court having jurisdiction in the place where the ward for the time being ordinarily resides; or
(c) in respect of any proceeding transferred under section 4A, the Court of the officer to whom such proceeding has been transferred.]
(6) "Collector" means the chief officer in charge of the revenue-administration of a district, and includes any officer whom the 3[Provincial Government], by notification in the official Gazette, may, by name or in virtue of his Office, appoint to be a Collector in any local area, or with respect to any class of persons, for all or any of the purposes of this Act:

lessons.title: Power to confer jurisdiction on subordinate judicial officers and to transfer proceedings to such officers
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Power to confer jurisdiction on subordinate judicial officers and to transfer proceedings to such officers' under The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 addresses 'Power to confer jurisdiction on subordinate judicial officers and to transfer proceedings to such officers'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
5[(1) The High Court may, by general or special order, empower any officer exercising original civil jurisdiction sub-ordinate to a District Court, or authorise the Judge of any District Court to empower any such officer subordinate to him, to dispose of any proceedings under this Act transferred to such officer under the provisions of this section.
(2) The Judge of a District Court may, by order in writing, transfer at any stage any proceeding under this Act pending in his Court for disposal to any officer subordinate to him empowered under sub-section (1).
(3) The Judge of a District Court may at any stage transfer to his own Court or to any officer subordinate to him empowered under sub-section (1) any proceeding under this Act pending in the Court of any other such officer.
(4) When any proceedings are transferred under this section in any case in which a guardian has been appointed or declared, the Judge of the District Court may, by order in writing, declare that the Court of the Judge or officer to whom they are transferred shall, for all or any of the purposes of this Act, be deemed to be the Court which appointed or declared the guardian.]

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. This section of The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 addresses 'Saving of power to appoint in other cases'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

lessons.title: Power of the Court to make order as to guardianship
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Power of the Court to make order as to guardianship' under The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Guardians and Wards Act, 1890 addresses 'Power of the Court to make order as to guardianship'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) Where the Court is satisfied that it is for the welfare of a minor that an order should be made-
(a) appointing a guardian of his person or property, or both, or
(b) declaring a person to be such a guardian, the Court may make an order accordingly.
(2) An order under this section shall imply the removal of any guardian who has not been appointed by will or other instrument or appointed or declared by the Court.
(3) Where a guardian has been appointed by will or other instrument or appointed or declared by the Court, an order under this section appointing or declaring another person to be guardian in his stead shall not be made until the powers of the guardian appointed or declared as aforesaid have ceased under the provisions of this Act.

lessons.title: Requisites to validity of Parsi marriages
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Requisites to validity of Parsi marriages' under The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Requisites to validity of Parsi marriages'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
No marriage shall be valid if-
(a) the contracting parties are related to each other in any of the degrees of consanguinity or affinity set forth in Schedule I; or
(b) such marriage is not solemnized according to the Parsi form of ceremony called "Ashirvad" by a priest in the presence of two Parsi witnesses other than such priest; or
(c) in the case of any Parsi (whether such Parsi has changed his or her religion or domicile or not) who has not completed the age of twenty-one years, the consent of his or her father or guardian has not been previously given to such marriage.

DETAILED EXPLANATION

lessons.title: Remarriage when unlawful
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Remarriage when unlawful' under The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Remarriage when unlawful'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) No Parsi (whether such Parsi has changed his or her religion or domicile or not) shall contract any marriage under this Act or any other law in the lifetime of his or her wife or husband, whether a Parsi or not, except after his or her lawful divorce from such wife or husband or after his or her marriage with such wife or husband has lawfully been declared null and void or dissolved, and, if the marriage was contracted with such wife or husband under the Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1865 (XV of 1865) or under this Act, except after a divorce, declaration or dissolution as aforesaid under either of the said Acts.
(2) Every marriage contracted contrary to the provisions of sub-section (1) shall be void.

lessons.title: Punishment of bigamy
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Punishment of bigamy' under The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Punishment of bigamy'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Every Parsi who during the lifetime of his or her wife or husband, whether a Parsi or not, contracts a marriage without having been lawfully divorced from such wife or husband, or without his or her marriage with such wife or husband having legally been declared null and void or dissolved, shall be subject to the penalties provided in sections 494 and 495 of the Pakistan Penal Code for the offence of marrying again during the lifetime of a husband or wife.

The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. lessons.title: Certificate and registry of marriage
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Certificate and registry of marriage' under The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Certificate and registry of marriage'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Every marriage contracted under this Act shall, immediately on the solemnization thereof, be certified by the officiating priest in the form contained in Schedule II. The certificate shall be signed by the said priest, the contracting parties, or their fathers or guardians when they shall not have completed the age of twenty-one years, and two witnesses present at the marriage; and the said priest shall thereupon send such certificate together with a fee of two rupees to be paid by the husband to the Registrar of the place at which such marriage is solemnized. The Registrar on receipt of the certificate and fee shall enter the certificate in a register to be kept by him for that purpose and shall be entitled to retain the fee.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

lessons.title: Appointment of Registrar
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Appointment of Registrar' under The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Appointment of Registrar'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
For the purposes of this Act a Registrar shall be appointed. Within the local limits of the ordinary original civil jurisdiction of a High Court, the Registrar shall be appointed by the Chief Justice of such Court, and without such limits, by the 1[Provincial Government]. Every Registrar so appointed may be removed by the Chief Justice or 1[Provincial Government] appointing him.

This section of The Parsi Marriage and Divorce Act, 1936 addresses 'Marriage registers to be open for public inspection'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
The register of marriages mentioned in section 6 shall, at all reasonable times, be open for inspection, and certified extracts therefrom shall, on application, be given by the Registrar on payment to him by the applicant of two rupees for each such extract. lessons.title: Short title, extent and commencement
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Short title, extent and commencement' under The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 addresses 'Short title, extent and commencement'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) This Act may be cited as the State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956.
(2) It extends to the whole of Pakistan.
(3) It shall come into force at once and except section 46, shall be deemed to have taken effect on and from the twelfth day of May 1948.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a business owner in Islamabad applying for a commercial loan. In this real-world scenario, 'Short title, extent and commencement' of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.
key_points: It extends to the whole of Pakistan.
It shall come into force at once and except section 46, shall be deemed to have taken effect on and from the twelfth day of May 1948.
This 'Short title, extent and commencement' provision is a binding part of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956

lessons.title: Establishment and incorporation of the Bank
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Establishment and incorporation of the Bank' under The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 addresses 'Establishment and incorporation of the Bank'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) As soon as may be after the commencement of this Act, steps shall be taken to establish, in accordance with the provisions of this Act, a bank to be called the State Bank of Pakistan or Bank-e-Daulat-e-Pakistan, for the purposes of taking over, as from the first day of July, 1948, the management of the currency from the Reserve Bank of India, and carrying on the business of Central Banking.
(2) The Bank shall be a body corporate by the name of the State Bank of Pakistan or Bank-e-Daulat-e-Pakistan, having perpetual succession and a common seal, and shall by the said name sue and be sued.
(3) Subject to this Act, the Bank shall have the power to acquire, hold and dispose of movable and immovable property of any kind, to enter into contracts and to undertake all activities necessary for the achievement of its objectives.

lessons.title: Share capital
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Share capital' under The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 addresses 'Share capital'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The authorized capital of the Bank shall be five hundred billion Rupees, divided into five billion shares of one hundred Rupees each. The authorized capital may be increased by the resolution of the Board, subject to the approval of the Federal Government.
(2) The paid-up capital of the Bank shall be one hundred billion Rupees, divided into one billion shares of one hundred Rupees each, which shall be made up through issuance of bonus shares by capitalizing of profits or general reserve or through subscription of shares in cash by the Federal Government.
(3) The capital shall be fully paid-up and held exclusively by the Federal Government and shall not be transferable to any other person or entity.
(4) The Board may, with the prior approval of the Federal Government, increase the paid-up capital from time to time by a resolution, which shall be made up in the same manner as specified in sub-section (2).
(5) No reduction in either the authorized capital or paid-up capital shall be made at any time.
(6) The nominal value, issue price, the manner in which the new shares may be issued and their assignment to the register of shareholders shall be determined by the Board.
(7) Notwithstanding the amount of paid-up capital prescribed in sub-sections (2), the paid-up capital and general reserves of the Bank shall increase to eight percent of its monetary liabilities through allocation from the distributable profit as determined in section 42.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

lessons.title: Re-capitalization
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Re-capitalization' under The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 addresses 'Re-capitalization'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
In the event that in the audited annual financial statements of the Bank, the sum of paid-up capital and general reserves falls below zero, then-
(a) the Board, with the advice of the external auditors of the Bank, shall examine and prepare a-report on the causes and extent of the shortfall within a period not exceeding thirty calendar days;
(b) in the event that the Board approves the report under clause (a), the Bank shall request the Federal Government for a capital contribution to remedy the deficit with a view to restoring the capital to the level of prescribed paid-up capital under section 4; and
(c) upon receipt of the request under clause (b), the Federal Government shall, within a period not exceeding thirty calendar days, transfer to the Bank the necessary amount in cash or in negotiable debt instruments with a specified maturity issued at prevailing market-related interest rates.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION

In this real-world scenario, 'Re-capitalization' of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

upon receipt of the request under clause (b), the Federal Government shall, within a period not exceeding thirty calendar days, transfer to the Bank the necessa

INTRODUCTION
This section of The State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 addresses 'Objectives'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The primary objective of the Bank shall be to achieve and maintain domestic price stability.
(2) Without prejudice to the Bank's primary objective, the Bank shall contribute to the stability of the financial system of Pakistan.
(3) Subject to sub-sections (1) and (2), the Bank shall support the Government's general economic policies with a view to contributing to fostering the development and fuller utilization of Pakistan's productive resources.

(2) The Bank may establish branches, offices, and agencies in Pakistan, or, with the prior approval of the Board anywhere outside Pakistan.

(a) to maintain an expert staff to study all questions of agricultural credit and be, available for consultation by the Federal Government, Provincial Governments, Provincial Co-operative Banks and other banking organisations ;

lessons.title: Act to override other laws
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Act to override other laws' under The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 addresses 'Act to override other laws'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) Except as provided in sub-section (2), this Act shall have effect notwithstanding anything contained in the Banks (Nationalization) Act, 1974 (XIX of 1974), or any other law, excepting the State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 (XXXIII of 1956), and the Banking Companies Ordinance, 1962 (LVII of 1962), as amended by this Act, for the time being in force or any instrument having effect by virtue of any law or any agreement, contract, award or memorandum or articles of association or other instrument.
(2) Nothing in- (a) section 36 of the State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 (XXXIII of 1956), shall apply to the Chairman or a Director, other than the Managing Director; (b) clause (iv) of sub-section (1) of section 14 of the Banking Companies Ordinance, 1962 (LVII of 1962), shall apply to shares of the Bank held by the State Bank; (c) section 27, section 28, clause (b) of sub-section (6) of section 40, clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 42, Parts III and IV, or section 89 of the Banking Companies Ordinance, 1962 (LVII of 1962), shall apply to the Bank; (d) clause (c) of sub-section (1), or sub-sections (2), (3), (4) and (5) of section 11, sub-section (3) of section 14, sections 15, 15A, 15B, 15C, or 20 sub-section (3) or (6) of section 25, sub-section (4) or (5) or section 40, or section 41A, 41B, 41C, or 83 of the Banking Companies Ordinance, 1962 (LVII of 1962), shall apply to the Chairman or a Director other than the Managing Director.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a business owner in Islamabad applying for a commercial loan. In this real-world scenario, 'Act to override other laws' of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

lessons.title: Power to exempt, and suspend operation of Act, in certain cases

You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 addresses 'Power to exempt, and suspend operation of Act, in certain cases'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
The Federal Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, and subject to such conditions as may be specified in the notification, exempt the Bank from, or suspend the application to the Bank of, all or any of the provisions of this Act, either generally or for such period as may be so specified.

lessons.title: Establishment of the Bank
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Establishment of the Bank' under The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 addresses 'Establishment of the Bank'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) As soon as may be after the coming into force of this Act, the Federal Government shall take steps to establish, in accordance with the provisions of this Act, a bank to be called "The Federal Bank for Cooperatives" to carry out the purposes of this Act.
(2) The Bank shall be a body corporate having perpetual succession and a common seal, with powers to acquire, own, hold and dispose of any property, and shall by the name assigned to it by sub-section (1) sue and be sued.
(3) The Bank shall be deemed to be a banking company for the purposes of the State Bank of Pakistan Act, 1956 (XXXIII of 1956), the Banking Companies Ordinance, 1962 (LVII of 1962), and any other law for the time being in force relating to banking companies, excepting the Banks (Nationalization) Act, 1974 (XIX of 1974).

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

In this real-world scenario, 'Establishment of the Bank' of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 as defined in the Act.

lessons.title: Share capital
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Share capital' under The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 addresses 'Share capital'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The initial share capital of the Bank shall be twenty crores of rupees divided into two thousand fully paid up shares of the nominal value of one hundred thousand rupees each and shall be fully subscribed by the Federal Government, the Provincial Governments and the State Bank of Pakistan in the following manner:- (a) Federal Government - Rs. 2.00 crores; (b) State Bank - Rs. 15.00 crores; (c) Government of the Punjab - Rs. 1.00 crores; (d) Government of Sind - Rs. 1.00 crores; (e) Government of N.W.F.P. - Rs. 0.50 crores; (f) Government of Baluchistan - Rs. 0.50 crores.
(2) The share capital may be increased from time to time by a resolution of the Board with the approval of the Federal Government, and the capital so increased shall be fully subscribed by the Federal Government, the Provincial Governments and the State Bank of Pakistan.

This section of The Establishment of the Federal Bank for Cooperatives and Regulation of Cooperative Banking Act, 1977 addresses 'Offices and branches'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)

(2) The Bank shall establish one Regional Office in each Province and may establish Offices, Branches and Agencies at such places as the Board may think fit.

lessons.title: Provision for enquiry in claims to land, or objections to sale of same
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Provision for enquiry in claims to land, or objections to sale of same' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Provision for enquiry in claims to land, or objections to sale of same'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
When any claim shall be preferred to any waste land proposed to be sold, or otherwise dealt with, on account of the Provincial Government, or when any objection shall be taken to the sale or other disposition of such land, the Collector of the district in which such land is situate, or other officer performing the duties of a Collector of Land Revenue in such district by whatever name his office is designated, shall, if the claim or objection be preferred within the period mentioned in the advertisement to be issued for the sale or other disposition of such land, which period shall not be less than three months, proceed to make an enquiry into the claim or objections.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION

In this real-world scenario, 'Provision for enquiry in claims to land, or objections to sale of same' of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

lessons.title: Procedure in such cases
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Procedure in such cases' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Procedure in such cases'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
The Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall call upon the claimant or objector to produce any evidence, or documents, upon which he may rely in proof of his claim or objection; and after considering the same, and making any further enquiry that may appear proper, shall dispose of the case by an order for the admission or rejection of the claim or objection; and if the land is proposed to be sold, for the sale of the same subject to any condition or reservation which, to such Collector or other officer as aforesaid, shall appear to be proper.

Notification of conditions. If the land is ordered to be sold subject to any condition or reservation, such condition or reservation shall be notified to intending purchasers at the time of sale.

You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Postponement of sale pending enquiry, to allow claimant to contest rejection of claim'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Pending an enquiry into any claim or objection under the last preceding section, the Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall postpone the sale or other disposition of the land;

and, if he shall order that such claim or objection be rejected, he shall further postpone the sale or other disposition of the land, to allow the claimant or objector to contest the order of rejection in the manner hereinafter provided.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a farmer in Sindh disputing a land title. In this real-world scenario, 'Postponement of sale pending enquiry, to allow claimant to contest rejection of claim' of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

This 'Postponement of sale pending enquiry, to allow claimant to contest rejection of claim' provision is a binding part of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863

lessons.title: Sale to be stopped if claim appear to be established, but may afterwards be proceeded with
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Sale to be stopped if claim appear to be established, but may afterwards be proceeded with' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Sale to be stopped if claim appear to be established, but may afterwards be proceeded with'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
If the Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall consider the claim or objection to be established, and that the sale or other disposition of the land should not take place, he shall stop the sale or other disposition of the land;

but such sale or other disposition of the land may afterwards be proceeded with, if, on an order issued to try the claim or objection, as provided in section 6 of this Act, the claimant or objector shall fail to establish the same.

lessons.title: Delivery to claimant of copy of order of rejection or of sale
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Delivery to claimant of copy of order of rejection or of sale' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Delivery to claimant of copy of order of rejection or of sale'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
If the Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall order that the claim or objection be rejected, or that the land be sold subject to any condition or reservation, or that it be otherwise dealt with, he shall cause a copy of such order to be delivered to the claimant or objector;

Order when final. and if such claimant or objector shall not, within one week from the delivery of such copy, or within such further time as the Collector or other officer as aforesaid, for any special reason to be recorded, shall see fit to grant, give notice in writing to such Collector or other officer as aforesaid that he intends to contest such order, the order shall be final.

Report to Board. If the claimant or objector shall, within the time allowed, give such notice, the Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall immediately make a report to the superior revenue authority to which he is immediately subordinate and shall forward with such report a copy of his order, stating fully all the circumstances of the case, and the evidence adduced in support, or otherwise, of the claim or objection;

Decision of Board. and such authority, on the receipt of such report, and after calling for any further information which it may consider necessary, may confirm, modify or reverse the order of the Collector or other officer as aforesaid.

Certification to Court. If the authority as aforesaid confirm the order of the Collector or other officer as aforesaid, or modify such order in such manner as to leave any part of such order in force adverse to the claimant or objector, the Collector or other officer as aforesaid shall certify such order to the court constituted or hereinafter provided;

Notice to Claimant. and such Court shall forthwith give notice to the claimant or objector;

Decision with final. and if such claimant or objector shall not institute a suit in such Court to establish his claim or objection, the order of the authority aforesaid shall be final.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. In this real-world scenario, 'Delivery to claimant of copy of order of rejection or of sale' of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

lessons.title: Power to order suit to try claim admitted by Collector
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Power to order suit to try claim admitted by Collector' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Power to order suit to try claim admitted by Collector'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
The Provincial Government may, within twelve months after the date on which the claim of any claimant of waste land, or the objection of any objector, as aforesaid, shall have been admitted under this Act by the Collector or other officer as aforesaid, direct a suit to be brought to try the claim or objection of the claimant or objector, in a Court constituted as hereinafter provided.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 as defined in the Act.

A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

lessons.title: Special Court for trying claims
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Special Court for trying claims' under The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 addresses 'Special Court for trying claims'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
For the investigation and trial of claims under this Act, the Provincial Government shall constitute, in every district in which there may be any waste lands capable of being sold, or otherwise dealt with, on account of the Provincial Government, a Court consisting of an uneven number of persons, not less than three, of whom the Judge of the district, or the officer presiding in the principal Civil Court of original jurisdiction in the district, by whatever name his office may be designated, shall be one.

Power of members. Any one or more of the members of which such Court shall consist shall have power to make all such orders in the case as may be necessary prior to the hearing of the suit:

Exclusion of officer making original enquiry. Provided that, whenever the Collector, or other officer, by whom the original enquiry was held, is the officer presiding in the principal Civil Court of original jurisdiction in the district, such officer shall not be a member of such Court.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a litigant filing a civil petition at the High Court. In this real-world scenario, 'Special Court for trying claims' of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of The Waste Lands (Claims) Act, 1863 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

lessons.title: Imposition of cess
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Imposition of cess' under The Agricultural Produce Cess Act, 1940. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of The Agricultural Produce Cess Act, 1940 addresses 'Imposition of cess'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) A customs duty at the rate of one-half of one per cent. ad valorem shall be levied on all articles included in the Schedule which are exported from Pakistan:

Provided that the said duty shall not be levied on articles proved to the satisfaction of the Collector not to have been produced in Pakistan:

Provided further that the Federal Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, vary the rate at which customs duty shall be levied.

(2) The Federal Government may, by notification in the official Gazette, fix for the purposes of levying the said duty tariff values of any articles included in the Schedule, and may alter any tariff values for the time being in force.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to The Agricultural Produce Cess Act, 1940. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

In this real-world scenario, 'Imposition of cess' of The Agricultural Produce Cess Act, 1940 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. INTRODUCTION
This section of The Agricultural Produce Cess Act, 1940 addresses 'Power to exclude articles from Schedule'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)

lessons.title: Penalty for causing disaffection, etc.
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Penalty for causing disaffection, etc.' under Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922 addresses 'Penalty for causing disaffection, etc.'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Whoever intentionally causes or attempts to cause, or does any act which he knows is likely to cause, disaffection towards Pakistan or the Government established by law in Pakistan amongst the members of a police-force, or induces or attempts to induce, or does any act which he knows is likely to induce, any member of a police-force to withhold his services or to commit a breach of discipline shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.

Explanation: Expressions of disapprobation of the measures of the Government with a view to obtain their alteration by lawful means, or of disapprobation of the administrative or other action of the Government, do not constitute an offence under this section unless they cause or are made for the purpose of causing or are likely to cause disaffection.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

lessons.title: Saving of acts done by police associations and other persons for certain purposes
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Saving of acts done by police associations and other persons for certain purposes' under Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922. lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922 addresses 'Saving of acts done by police associations and other persons for certain purposes'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
Nothing shall be deemed to be an offence under this Act which is done in good faith-
(a) for the purpose of promoting the welfare or interest of any member of a police-force by inducing him to withhold his services in any manner, authorised, by law; or
(b) by or on behalf of any association formed for the purpose of furthering the interests of members of a police-force as such, where the association has been authorised or recognised by the Government and the act done is done under any rules or articles of the association which have been approved by the Government.

This section of Police (Incitement to Disaffection) Act 1922 addresses 'Trial of cases'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)

(2) Notwithstanding anything contained in Chapter XXII of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 (V of 1898), no offence under this Act shall be triable summarily.

INTRODUCTION
This section of Airports Security Force Act 1975 addresses 'Power to constitute and maintain the Airports Security Force and its functions'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The Federal Government may constitute and maintain a force to be called the Airport Security Force for performing the following functions: ensuring security of all airports, aerodromes, aircraft and civil aviation installations; ensuring security of structures and installations; proper conduct of persons at airports; security of passengers, baggage, cargo and mail; maintenance of law and order; and such other functions as required.
(2) Officers and members shall act in accordance with this Act and the rules and regulations.

(2) The Force shall consist of such ranks and number of officers and members as prescribed by rules.
(3) Officers and members shall receive prescribed pay, allowances, pensions, leave, and privileges.
(4) On appointment, each officer and member shall receive a certificate vesting them with powers and privileges.
(5) Persons ceasing to be officers or members shall surrender the certificate.

lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of Pakistan Railways Police Act 1977 addresses 'Power to constitute, control and maintain the Pakistan Railways Police and its functions'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The Federal Government may constitute, control and maintain a force to be called the Pakistan Railways Police for performing functions within specified territorial limits: preventing loss to Railway property, ensuring proper conduct of persons on Railways, maintaining law and order, assisting passengers and railway employees, protecting travellers, keeping platforms clear of idlers, searching empty carriages, regulating vehicle movement, reporting violations, and other functions as required.
(2) The Federal Government may exercise all powers exercisable by a Provincial Government under the Police Act, 1861 and the Code in relation to the Provincial Police.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to Pakistan Railways Police Act 1977. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

INTRODUCTION
This section of Pakistan Railways Police Act 1977 addresses 'Constitution superintendence and administration of the Pakistan Railways Police'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

INTRODUCTION
This section of Pakistan Railways Police Act 1977 addresses 'Transfer of Provincial Railway Police and Railway Watch and Ward to the Pakistan Railways Police'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
All persons holding any rank or post, not above Superintendent, in the Provincial Railway Police and all persons in Railway Watch and Ward are transferred to corresponding posts in the Pakistan Railways Police subject to Federal Government conditions. LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) As soon as may be after the commencement of this Ordinance, the Federal Government shall, by notification in the official Gazette, establish a Board to be known as the Pakistan Madrassah Education Board.

lessons.title: Composition of the Board
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Composition of the Board' under Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 addresses 'Composition of the Board'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The Board shall consist of the following members, to be appointed by the Federal Government, namely: (i) An educationist of eminence - Chairman; (ii) Sadr or nazim of a wafaq or tanzim or rabitah to be appointed by the Federal Government in consultation with the Chairman - Vice-Chairman; (iii) an educationist having experience in administrative matters - Secretary; (iv) Secretary, Ministry of Education or his nominee not below the rank of a Joint Secretary - Member; (v) Secretary, Ministry of Religious Affairs or his nominee not below the rank of a Joint Secretary - Member; (vi) Secretary, Ministry of Science and Technology or his nominee not below the rank of a Joint Secretary - Member; (vii) Chairman, University Grants Commission or a Member nominated by him - Member; (viii) two Ulema who are or have been members of the Council of Islamic Ideology to be nominated by the Chairman - Members; (ix) Director General, Dawa Academy, International Islamic University - Member; (x) one Professor who is also a head of the Department of Islamic Studies in a University - Member; (xi) Provincial Education Secretaries of all the four Provinces - Members; (xii) one President or Nazim of Wifaqs - Members; (xiii) one President or Nazim of Tanzim - Member; (xiv) one President or Nazim of Rabita - Member; (xv) Chairman, Inter-Board Co-ordination Committee (IBCC) - Member; (xvi) two representatives of the Ministry of Science and Technology - Members.
(2) A member, other than an ex officio member, unless earlier removed shall hold office during the pleasure of the Federal Government.
(3) No person shall be appointed or continue to be a member of the Board if he has been declared insolvent or is convicted of an offence involving moral turpitude or is declared disqualified from employment in, or dismissed from, Government service.
(4) No person, who has committed misconduct, directly or indirectly, or he himself or his partner has any share or interest in a contract with, by or on behalf of the Board or is in any employment under, by or on behalf of the Board, shall be appointed or continue on the Board. On receipt of any such complaint against a member, the Board, after giving the member an opportunity of hearing, shall decide the matter, and send its recommendation to the Federal Government for its approval. The decision of the Federal Government shall be final.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a religious institution seeking government affiliation. In this real-world scenario, 'Composition of the Board' of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

KEY LEGAL QUESTIONS
Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
A: This provision operates within the broader framework of Pakistani law. Where conflicts arise, courts apply principles of statutory interpretation to determine the applicable rule.

No person shall be appointed or continue to be a member of the Board if he has been declared insolvent or is convicted of an offence involving moral turpitude o
No person, who has committed misconduct, directly or indirectly, or he himself or his partner has any share or interest in a contract with, by or on behalf of t
This 'Composition of the Board' provision is a binding part of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001

lessons.title: Powers and functions of the Board
lessons.summary: This lesson covers 'Powers and functions of the Board' under Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001. You will learn the legal requirements, rights, and obligations established in this section, and understand how it applies in real Pakistani legal scenarios.
lessons.content:
INTRODUCTION
This section of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 addresses 'Powers and functions of the Board'. It is an important provision that defines rights, duties, and procedures under this Pakistani law.

LEGAL PROVISION (Full Text)
(1) The Board shall have powers to perform such functions and exercise such powers as are necessary or conducive to implement and achieve the purposes of this Ordinance.
(2) Subject to the provisions of this Ordinance and with due regard to the autonomy of a Wifaq or Tanzeem or Rabita, the Board shall have the following powers and functions to perform: (a) to establish such model madaris and model darul ulooms in which the Islamic Education is the main component and includes courses and curricula of the general education system as may be prescribed; (b) to regulate and approve conditions for affiliation of such existing madaris as have duly been recommended by the Academic Council; (c) to approve the curricula, courses of study and conditions for holding of examinations in the model dini madaris keeping in view the recommendations of the Academic Council; (d) to organize teachers training programmes for the teachers of the model dini madaris and dar-ul-ulooms in various social sciences and religious courses; (e) to grant affiliation and to lay down the regulations and to approve conditions for affiliation of existing madaris; (f) to suggest ways and means to bridge the existing gulf between the general education system and the madaris by revising and improving the curricula of Madaris and general education programs; (g) to approve the curricula, courses of study and the conditions for holding of examinations in the Dini Madaris on recommendations of the Academic Council; (h) to appoint such officers and staff as it may consider necessary for the performance of its functions and define their duties and lay down conditions of service; (i) to promote co-operation and co-ordination amongst existing Dini Madaris and their organizations; (j) to approve equivalence of degrees, diplomas, sanads or certificates where curricula includes subjects of the general education system; (k) to promote curricular and extra curricula activities in Model Dini Madaris and Model Darul Ulooms; (l) to inspect or arrange for inspection of a Model Dini Madrasah or Model Darul Uloom with a view to improve the performance of such Madrasah or the Darul Uloom; (m) to institute and award scholarships, medals and prizes subject to such conditions as may be prescribed; (n) to assist in the development of libraries of Dini Madaris; (o) to approve the Annual Budget; (p) to advise the Government in matters pertaining to model Dini Madaris; (q) to prescribe minimum conditions for admission of candidates to the examinations conducted by the Board and to print all educational material necessary for improving the standard of education; (r) to regulate and decide all administrative matters including creation and abolition of posts under the Board; (s) to enter into or carry out or cancel any contract to administer, invest buy or sell any moveable or immoveable property and assets; (t) to spend or incur expenditure as may be necessary for carrying out purposes of this Ordinance; (u) the Board may delegate any of its functions and powers to any member or officer of the Board or a committee or a sub-committee appointed by it; (v) to hear complaints, conduct enquiries and inspections and direct or recommend measures for the improvement of any dini madaris established by or affiliated to the Board; and (w) to exercise inter alia all powers as given in this Ordinance and to perform all other acts necessary for carrying out the purposes of this Ordinance.

DETAILED EXPLANATION
The above provision establishes specific legal requirements that must be followed by all parties subject to Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001. It creates a binding framework that governs the conduct of individuals, organisations, and government bodies in this area. Courts in Pakistan interpret this section when adjudicating disputes arising under this legislation. Understanding the exact wording and scope of this provision is essential for legal practice and informed citizenship.

PRACTICAL APPLICATION
Consider a religious institution seeking government affiliation. In this real-world scenario, 'Powers and functions of the Board' of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 would directly govern the rights and obligations of the parties. They must comply with the requirements set out in this section to protect their legal interests, enforce their rights, or avoid penalties.

Q: Who is bound by this provision?
A: All persons and entities within the jurisdiction of Pakistan Madrasah Education (Establishment and Affiliation of Model Dini Madaris) Board Ordinance 2001 as defined in the Act.

Q: What are the consequences of non-compliance?
A: Non-compliance may result in legal action, penalties, loss of rights, or other consequences as prescribed in this legislation.

Q: How does this section interact with other laws?
